questions.question: A team finds that a faulty sensor consistently recorded temperatures a few degrees too high, skewing the dataset used to train a model. Which type of bias does this flawed measurement introduce?

choices.text: Confirmation bias, where humans favor information confirming their beliefs.
choices.explanation: Confirmation bias is a human cognitive bias toward supporting evidence. A faulty sensor is a measurement problem, not confirmation bias.

choices.text: Observer bias, where the person interpreting data influences results.
choices.explanation: Observer bias stems from human interpretation. The described issue is a malfunctioning measurement tool, which is measurement bias.

choices.text: Sampling bias, where the data does not represent the full population.
choices.explanation: Sampling bias is about an unrepresentative selection of data. Here the selection may be fine, but the measurement instrument is faulty, which is measurement bias.

choices.text: Measurement bias, from flawed collection tools or instruments.
choices.explanation: Measurement bias arises when the instruments or methods used to collect data are flawed, such as a miscalibrated sensor, systematically distorting the data the model learns from.

questions.question: A team deploys a SageMaker model that receives sporadic, unpredictable traffic with idle periods, and they do not want to manage or pay for always-on servers. They can tolerate occasional cold starts. Which SageMaker deployment option fits best?

choices.text: Edge inference on user devices.
choices.explanation: Edge inference runs locally on devices for offline or low-latency needs. It does not address serverless, intermittent cloud traffic with no server management.

choices.text: A always-on real-time endpoint provisioned for peak load at all times.
choices.explanation: A constantly provisioned real-time endpoint adds cost during idle periods, which is wasteful for sporadic traffic. Serverless inference better matches intermittent demand.

choices.text: Batch transform running continuously.
choices.explanation: Batch transform is for bulk offline processing, not for serving sporadic real-time requests. It does not fit an on-demand inference need.

choices.text: Serverless inference, for intermittent traffic with cold starts.
choices.explanation: SageMaker Serverless Inference automatically provisions and scales capacity for intermittent traffic without managing servers, and is appropriate when occasional cold starts are acceptable.

questions.question: A penetration tester crafts inputs that trick a model into revealing its hidden system prompt and confidential instructions. Which generative AI risk does this represent?

choices.text: Prompt leaking, where hidden prompts or context are exposed.
choices.explanation: Prompt leaking is when hidden system prompts or sensitive context are exposed through crafted inputs. Mitigations include avoiding secrets in prompts, limiting context, and using filters.

choices.text: Toxicity, the generation of offensive content.
choices.explanation: Toxicity is about offensive output. Revealing a hidden system prompt is prompt leaking, not toxicity.

choices.text: Overfitting, a training generalization problem.
choices.explanation: Overfitting concerns poor generalization from training. It is not an attack that exposes a model's hidden prompt.

choices.text: Data poisoning, where malicious data is added to training data.
choices.explanation: Data poisoning corrupts the training data. The scenario is about extracting hidden prompt content at inference time, which is prompt leaking.

questions.question: A medical screening classifier must correctly identify patients who are truly healthy (true negatives) to avoid unnecessary follow-up procedures. Which metric measures the proportion of actual negatives the model correctly rejects?

choices.text: R-squared, a regression metric.
choices.explanation: R-squared describes variance explained in regression. It is not a measure of correctly identified negatives in classification.

choices.text: Recall, the proportion of actual positives the model identifies.
choices.explanation: Recall focuses on actual positive cases found, not negatives correctly rejected. The metric for correctly identified negatives is specificity.

choices.text: Specificity, the share of actual negatives correctly identified.
choices.explanation: Specificity measures, of all the actual negative cases, how many the model correctly classifies as negative. It is the right metric when correctly identifying true negatives matters.

choices.text: RMSE, a regression error metric.
choices.explanation: RMSE measures error in continuous regression predictions. It does not apply to a classification true-negative rate.

questions.question: A developer provides exactly one example of the desired input-and-output format in the prompt, then asks the model to handle a new input the same way. Which prompting technique is this?

choices.text: Zero-shot prompting, which provides no examples.
choices.explanation: Zero-shot prompting includes no examples at all. Providing exactly one example is one-shot prompting.

choices.text: Few-shot prompting, which provides several examples.
choices.explanation: Few-shot prompting uses multiple examples. A single example is specifically one-shot prompting.

choices.text: One-shot prompting, which provides a single guiding example.
choices.explanation: One-shot prompting supplies a single example to demonstrate the desired format or style before the real task. It sits between zero-shot (no examples) and few-shot (several examples).

choices.text: Chain-of-thought prompting, which elicits step-by-step reasoning.
choices.explanation: Chain-of-thought asks the model to reason through steps. Showing one example of format is one-shot prompting, not chain-of-thought.

questions.question: A manufacturer wants to detect their own specific products and brand logos on store shelves in images, which generic image labels cannot identify. Which capability should they use?

choices.text: Amazon Rekognition Custom Labels, a custom vision model on your images.
choices.explanation: Rekognition Custom Labels lets you train a custom computer vision model using your own labeled images to detect domain-specific objects such as particular products or brand logos.

choices.text: Amazon Transcribe custom vocabulary.
choices.explanation: Transcribe custom vocabulary improves speech-to-text for specific words. It cannot detect products or logos in images.

choices.text: Amazon Comprehend custom classification.
choices.explanation: Comprehend custom classification works on text documents, not images. Detecting logos in images requires a vision service like Rekognition.

choices.text: Amazon Translate custom terminology.
choices.explanation: Custom terminology guides translation of specific terms. It has nothing to do with detecting objects in images.

questions.question: A Bedrock agent needs to run custom business logic, such as calling an internal order system, as part of completing a task. Which AWS service is commonly used to implement that action logic for the agent?

choices.text: Amazon Polly, a text-to-speech service.
choices.explanation: Amazon Polly generates speech from text. It does not run custom business logic for an agent's actions.

choices.text: AWS Trusted Advisor, an account recommendation service.
choices.explanation: Trusted Advisor provides best-practice recommendations. It does not run code for a Bedrock agent's action groups.

choices.text: AWS Lambda, which runs the function code a Bedrock agent invokes.
choices.explanation: AWS Lambda is commonly used to implement the action logic a Bedrock agent invokes, such as calling internal systems. It is serverless and event-driven, making it well suited to agent action groups and AI automation.

choices.text: Amazon Macie, a sensitive data discovery service.
choices.explanation: Amazon Macie discovers sensitive data in S3. It is not used to execute an agent's action logic.

questions.question: A team wants a model that can take a text prompt and generate an original image, and also analyze an image and answer questions about it. Which characterization is most accurate?

choices.text: Both tasks are handled by k-means clustering.
choices.explanation: K-means groups unlabeled data into clusters. It cannot generate images or answer questions about them.

choices.text: Both tasks require a simple linear regression model.
choices.explanation: Linear regression predicts a single continuous value and cannot generate images or reason over image-and-text inputs. These are multimodal generative tasks.

choices.text: Neither task is possible with current AI models.
choices.explanation: Both text-to-image generation and visual question answering are well-established capabilities of modern diffusion and multimodal models.

choices.text: Multimodal capabilities, using diffusion and vision-language models.
choices.explanation: Working across text and images is multimodal. Text-to-image generation commonly relies on diffusion models, while answering questions about an image uses a multimodal model that processes both vision and language.

questions.question: A team wants to use AWS's own family of foundation models, available through Amazon Bedrock, as one of their model options. Which of the following is an AWS foundation model family?

choices.text: AWS Config, a configuration compliance service.
choices.explanation: AWS Config tracks resource configuration and compliance. It is not a foundation model offering.

choices.text: Amazon Titan, an AWS foundation model family on Amazon Bedrock.
choices.explanation: Amazon Titan is an AWS foundation model family offered through Amazon Bedrock, alongside Amazon Nova and models from other providers, giving customers AWS-built model options.

choices.text: Amazon Inspector, a vulnerability scanning service.
choices.explanation: Amazon Inspector scans workloads for vulnerabilities. It is not a foundation model family.

choices.text: Amazon Kendra, an enterprise search service.
choices.explanation: Amazon Kendra provides document search. It is not a foundation model family.

questions.question: A company building a customer-facing AI assistant wants to act responsibly. Which combination of practices reflects responsible AI principles? (Select all that apply.)

choices.text: Protect user privacy and secure personal and sensitive data.
choices.explanation: Privacy and security, protecting personal and sensitive data, is a foundational responsible AI dimension.

choices.text: Design for controllability so the system stays aligned with human intent and can be overridden.
choices.explanation: Controllability, keeping AI aligned with human intent and allowing human oversight or override, is a recognized responsible AI dimension.

choices.text: Collect additional personal data for personalization without defining a purpose, retention period, or user notice.
choices.explanation: Personalization can be legitimate, but collecting extra personal data without a defined purpose, retention policy, or notice conflicts with privacy and data-minimization principles.

choices.text: Describe AI limitations only in internal documentation, without user-facing disclosure.
choices.explanation: Internal documentation helps governance, but responsible AI also requires appropriate transparency to users. Users should understand when AI is involved and what important limitations apply.

choices.text: Be transparent with users about AI involvement and the system's limitations.
choices.explanation: Transparency, including disclosing AI involvement and being honest about limitations, is a core responsible AI principle that supports informed, trustworthy use.
